content: Hey Saturday, I've been thinking about how we should work together. I'm pretty particular about communication style, and I want to set some expectations upfront. I work best in the mornings, like before 10 AM, so that's when I'll be reaching out to you most. Also, I really prefer short, direct answers without a bunch of bullet points or lists. Can you do that for me?

Absolutely, I can work with that. I hear you on the morning preference—there's something about tackling things early that just hits different. I'll keep that in mind when you come to me, and I'll make sure my responses are concise and flowing naturally rather than breaking everything into bullet points. That actually makes conversations feel more like talking to a real person anyway. What else should I know about how you prefer to interact?

Great, thanks. One more thing—when you're helping me decide something, don't throw like five different options at me. I get paralyzed by too many choices and it actually makes me more anxious. Give me maybe one or two solid suggestions, and if I want more, I'll ask for them. Also, ask me one question at a time instead of three or four. Does that make sense?

That makes total sense. I know that feeling of choice overload, and you're right that it can be counterproductive. I'll stick to giving you one or two strong recommendations with my reasoning, and I'll ask follow-up questions one at a time so the conversation flows naturally. It's more respectful of your time and mental space anyway. So when you're working through decisions with me, you'll get focused advice rather than a menu of possibilities.

Perfect. Okay, so I'm dealing with this work situation that's been bugging me. My manager asked me to take on this new project alongside my current workload, and I honestly don't think I have the bandwidth. I've already got three major deliverables due in the next three weeks. How would you approach this conversation with my manager?

The key here is going in with specifics rather than just saying you're busy. I'd suggest laying out your current workload with actual deadlines—literally name those three deliverables and when they're due—then ask your manager which of these takes priority or if something gets pushed. That frames it as a resource conversation, not you pushing back. You're basically saying "I want to do good work on all of this, so help me understand what matters most right now."

That's smart. I like that approach because it puts the ball back in their court without being confrontational. Should I send an email first or just catch them in person? I'm thinking about doing this tomorrow morning since that's when I think clearest and my manager usually gets in early too.

Tomorrow morning is good timing since you'll both be fresh. I'd lean toward an in-person conversation rather than email—it's harder to misinterpret tone and it shows you're taking it seriously by having a real discussion. Plus with something potentially contentious, email can sit wrong in someone's inbox. Just schedule ten minutes on their calendar if you can, or catch them early before the day gets hectic.

Okay, I'll try to grab him around 8:30 AM before our team standup. One more thing though—I'm worried about coming across as someone who can't handle a full workload. How do I make sure he doesn't think I'm being lazy or not up for the challenge?

Mention what you've already accomplished on your current projects rather than just listing what you have to do. Say something like "I want to make sure I deliver the same quality you've come to expect on the Morrison project and the Q2 review," and then explain you're trying to be realistic about timeline. That shows you care about quality and you're being strategic, not that you're avoiding work. Most managers actually respect that kind of honesty.

That makes sense. I feel a lot more confident about this now. By the way, what time do you think would be ideal for me to start using you for help with other projects? I mentioned I'm most productive in the mornings, so should I only reach out then, or does it matter from your perspective?

From my end there's no difference—I'm always available whenever you need me. But if you work best in the mornings, that's probably when you should actually be doing the work rather than asking me questions about it. The real value is you reaching out when you're already in a productive headspace and ready to implement whatever we talk through. So use me whenever you need to, but yeah, morning conversations probably stick with you better.

That's a good point. Actually, I've been meaning to ask about something else. I need to update my resume for some positions I'm looking at, and I'm not even sure where to start. It's been like five years since I've seriously looked for a job. What's the first thing I should do?

Start by actually getting your old resume and reading through it like you're seeing it for the first time. You'll probably spot things that are outdated or don't reflect where you are now, which gives you a baseline. That usually takes less than an hour and it's way better than staring at a blank page trying to figure out where to begin. Once you've done that, you'll have a sense of what needs to change.

Okay, I'll dig that up tonight. One question though—should I be tailoring my resume for each job I apply to, or is one good version enough to send to multiple places? I want to be efficient about this since I've got limited free time.

You should have a good master version, but then tweak the bullet points slightly for different roles, especially the skills section. It doesn't take long once you have the base there, and it makes a real difference because hiring managers scan for specific keywords. The efficient way is to keep your master version and just adjust maybe three or four bullet points per application rather than rewriting the whole thing. Give yourself an hour per application tops and you'll be fine.

That's helpful. Hey, I'm curious—do you have any specific advice on what companies or industries are hiring right now, or is that outside your wheelhouse? I've been in tech for about eight years, mostly in product management, but I'm open to shifting directions a bit.

I can definitely talk through what I know, though my information has a cutoff date so some specifics might be outdated. That said, product management skills are pretty transferable and there's steady demand across fintech, healthcare tech, and even traditional companies modernizing their digital products. Before I rattle off a bunch of stuff though, what's pulling you toward a potential shift—are you burned out on tech entirely, or just looking for a different type of product work?

Honestly, I'm not burned out on tech itself, but I've been doing SaaS products focused on enterprise stuff, and it's kind of soulless. I want to work on something where I can actually see the impact. Like something in education, healthcare, or maybe climate tech. Do any of those feel realistic as a pivot?

All three are realistic with your background, actually. Education tech and healthcare tech both need people who understand product management from a business perspective, and your eight years in SaaS is valuable there. Climate tech is probably the most crowded and competitive right now, but it's definitely hiring. I'd say education or healthcare is your best bet because the roles are more abundant and companies are actively looking for people who can bring that enterprise discipline you already have. What matters more to you—the industry itself or the specific type of product work you'd be doing?

The industry matters a lot because I need to feel like I'm contributing to something bigger than making sure users click more buttons. Healthcare probably appeals to me more than education, just because I think the stakes feel higher. So if I'm targeting healthcare tech companies, should I adjust my resume specifically to highlight relevant experience, or would that feel disingenuous since I haven't worked in healthcare before?

You don't need to fake healthcare experience, but you can frame your existing work in ways that show you understand complex user needs and regulation. If you've ever dealt with compliance, data privacy, or situations where user mistakes had real consequences, lean into those stories on your resume. Healthcare companies care less about whether you've worked in their industry and more about whether you can handle complexity and care about getting things right. Your eight years of enterprise product work actually proves that better than healthcare-specific experience would anyway.
